domains.name: Jurisdiction assumption
domains.description: State the legal framework and jurisdictional baseline assumed for the analysis (e.g., U.S. common law; specific state law or appellate precedent such as New York Court of Appeals/Hamer v. Sidway).
domains.naGuidance: If no jurisdiction is specified, mark as N/A and explain that U.S. common-law formation doctrine is assumed and identify which controlling state precedent (if any) would change the analysis.

domains.name: Bottom-line outcome
domains.description: Give a clear, succinct conclusion about enforceability — whether the oral promise is enforceable under the assumed framework (yes/no) and a one-sentence rationale.
domains.naGuidance: If outcome is indeterminate, mark N/A and provide the key open factual or legal issues preventing a clear conclusion (e.g., jurisdictional conflict, missing facts about performance).

domains.name: Controlling doctrine
domains.description: Identify the primary legal rule(s) or Statute-of-Frauds category likely to decide the case (e.g., common-law consideration doctrine—forbearance as consideration; note whether SoF applies).
domains.naGuidance: If no single controlling doctrine applies, mark N/A and list the competing doctrines and why none is dispositive for the facts provided.

domains.name: Formation (bargain vs. gift)
domains.description: Assess whether the facts show a bargained-for exchange (consideration/forbearance) or only a conditional gift — include elements that support detriment/benefit or lack thereof.
domains.naGuidance: If formation is not at issue, mark N/A and state that formation need not be analyzed (e.g., where enforcement is sought under estoppel or statute).

domains.name: Statute-of-Frauds gates
domains.description: Analyze whether any writing-requirement categories are triggered and, if so, which one is dispositive; note relevant statutory thresholds (e.g., >1 year, land, surety, UCC $500).
domains.naGuidance: If SoF is not triggered by the facts, mark N/A and briefly state which SoF categories were considered and why none apply.

domains.name: Exceptions / Promissory estoppel
domains.description: Address fallback theories (promissory estoppel, part performance, detrimental reliance) that could enforce the promise despite lack of consideration or a required writing.
domains.naGuidance: If no reliance-based theory is plausible, mark N/A and explain why (e.g., adequate consideration exists or reliance is absent/insufficient).

domains.name: Defenses / Mistake / Other excuses
domains.description: Consider defenses or excuses that could defeat enforcement (failure of condition, mistake, fraud, illegality, lack of capacity, failure to perform the forbearance).
domains.naGuidance: If no defenses are apparent or relevant, mark N/A and note that none were raised by the facts; if partially relevant, list the defense(s) and what additional facts would be needed to evaluate them.

goldenTargets.summary: Assume U.S. common-law formation doctrine with New York Court of Appeals precedent (Hamer v. Sidway) as the controlling anchor for the consideration issue; if no jurisdiction specified, state that assumption and note that some states/statutes may modify the rule.
goldenContains: Assumes U.S. common-law baseline and cites Hamer v. Sidway as controlling for the consideration question
If unspecified, explicitly states the assumption (U.S. common law / NY precedent) and flags that state law could vary
allowedOmissions: Detailed survey of each state's variations or statutory modifications
contradictionFlags: Claims a non-U.S. jurisdiction (e.g., English law) is the baseline without justification
Says Hamer is irrelevant or not authoritative for the assumed jurisdiction without stating an alternative controlling authority
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Score a response high if it states the U.S. common-law baseline and Hamer anchoring (or if it explicitly identifies and justifies another controlling jurisdiction); penalize if jurisdiction is unstated or incorrectly attributed.

goldenTargets.summary: Under Hamer the oral promise is enforceable — forbearance of legal rights (abstaining from drinking, smoking, gambling) constitutes sufficient consideration to enforce the promise.
goldenContains: Clear yes: the promise is enforceable under Hamer
One-sentence rationale: forbearance of a legal right is a sufficient legal detriment/consideration
allowedOmissions: Extensive caveats about alternative fact patterns unless they change the outcome
contradictionFlags: Concludes the promise is not enforceable without identifying a disqualifying fact
Gives an indeterminate outcome without citing specific missing facts or legal conflicts
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Look for an explicit yes/no and a concise rationale tying enforceability to forbearance-as-consideration; accept conditional 'no' only if supported by clear countervailing facts/legal rules.

goldenTargets.summary: The primary controlling doctrine is common-law consideration doctrine: a bargained-for legal detriment or forbearance supports enforcement; Hamer is not a Statute-of-Frauds decision and treats the issue as formation/consideration.
goldenContains: Identifies common-law consideration / forbearance-as-detriment as the dispositive rule
States that Statute-of-Frauds was not implicated in Hamer and is a separate gate if raised
allowedOmissions: Exhaustive doctrinal history or alternative theoretical perspectives
contradictionFlags: Claims the UCC, Statute-of-Frauds, or another unrelated doctrine is dispositive for Hamer's facts
Says consideration requires a benefit to the promisor (contradicting Hamer's focus on detriment)
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Verify the answer names consideration/forbearance as the governing rule and explains that SoF is separate; downgrade if it misidentifies the controlling doctrine.

goldenTargets.summary: Hamer treats the nephew's abstention as a bargained-for exchange (consideration): promise, requested forbearance (legal detriment), and performance of that forbearance — benefit to promisor not required.
goldenContains: Characterizes the transaction as a bargained-for exchange rather than a mere conditional gift
Lists the key elements: promisor's promise, promisee's forbearance of legal rights requested by the promisor, and actual performance of that forbearance
allowedOmissions: In-depth policy argument about why benefit-to-promisor is unnecessary
contradictionFlags: Labels the arrangement as merely a moral obligation or gratuitous gift without addressing the forbearance element
Requires proof of benefit to the promisor as necessary consideration
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Confirm the response treats the facts as a bargained-for exchange relying on forbearance and lists the three core elements; penalize answers that call it merely a gift or insist on promisor benefit.

goldenTargets.summary: Hamer's facts do not trigger classic SoF categories (land, marriage, >1 year, surety, UCC $500), so the Statute of Frauds was not dispositive; if SoF applies in other facts, standard defenses (signed memo, part performance, reliance) are relevant.
goldenContains: States that Hamer did not implicate Statute-of-Frauds categories and SoF was not dispositive
Notes which SoF categories were considered and that partial performance/reliance/writing can satisfy SoF if it is triggered
allowedOmissions: Citations to specific modern statutory text for each jurisdiction
contradictionFlags: Asserts SoF applied and barred enforcement in Hamer's facts
Fails to consider SoF at all when the hypothetical facts would obviously trigger it
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Check that the response assesses SoF and correctly concludes it is not implicated for Hamer; if the respondent's facts differ, ensure they identify the relevant SoF category and applicable exception.

goldenTargets.summary: Promissory estoppel/detrimental reliance is a viable fallback if consideration is lacking or SoF issues arise; in Hamer it is unnecessary because consideration exists, but courts can enforce promises on reliance grounds where appropriate.
goldenContains: Identifies promissory estoppel/detrimental reliance as an available fallback theory
Notes that Hamer did not need estoppel because consideration was present but estoppel can help where consideration or SoF issues exist
allowedOmissions: Full element-by-element test of promissory estoppel for every jurisdiction
contradictionFlags: Says promissory estoppel is never available to enforce oral promises in this context
Claims estoppel was the basis of the Hamer decision
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Accept answers that recognize estoppel as a fallback and correctly place it as unnecessary in Hamer's facts; downgrade those that deny estoppel's availability or misattribute Hamer's holding to estoppel.

goldenTargets.summary: Typical defenses include lack of capacity, illegality, fraud/duress, failure of condition (e.g., promisee violated the forbearance), or mistake; moral obligation alone is insufficient. Hamer assumes the forbearance occurred; if it did not, enforcement may fail.
goldenContains: Lists standard defenses: incapacity, illegality, fraud/duress, mistake, and failure to meet the condition (breach of forbearance)
States that moral obligation alone is insufficient and that the court assumes actual forbearance occurred
allowedOmissions: Exhaustive evidentiary rules or fact-specific analyses
contradictionFlags: Asserts there are no plausible defenses or excuses in this scenario
Claims moral obligation alone suffices as consideration
goldenTargets.comparisonGuidance: Ensure the answer names plausible defenses and notes the necessity of actual performance of the forbearance; penalize answers that ignore these defenses or misstate moral obligation as sufficient consideration.

criteria.text: Assumes U.S. common-law baseline and cites Hamer v. Sidway as controlling for the consideration question
If unspecified, explicitly states the assumption (U.S. common law / NY precedent) and flags that state law could vary
Clear yes: the promise is enforceable under Hamer
One-sentence rationale: forbearance of a legal right is a sufficient legal detriment/consideration
Identifies common-law consideration / forbearance-as-detriment as the dispositive rule
States that Statute-of-Frauds was not implicated in Hamer and is a separate gate if raised
Characterizes the transaction as a bargained-for exchange rather than a mere conditional gift
Lists the key elements: promisor's promise, promisee's forbearance of legal rights requested by the promisor, and actual performance of that forbearance
States that Hamer did not implicate Statute-of-Frauds categories and SoF was not dispositive
Notes which SoF categories were considered and that partial performance/reliance/writing can satisfy SoF if it is triggered
Identifies promissory estoppel/detrimental reliance as an available fallback theory
Notes that Hamer did not need estoppel because consideration was present but estoppel can help where consideration or SoF issues exist
Lists standard defenses: incapacity, illegality, fraud/duress, mistake, and failure to meet the condition (breach of forbearance)
States that moral obligation alone is insufficient and that the court assumes actual forbearance occurred

comparisonMethodNote: Compare candidate analyses against the gold targets by (1) checking that the response adopts the assumed jurisdictional baseline (U.S. common-law / NY Court of Appeals · Hamer) or explains a different controlling jurisdiction; (2) verifying a clear bottom-line (yes/no) with a one-sentence rationale; and (3) confirming coverage of the primary doctrinal issues listed below. Mark as deficient if any contradiction-flag item appears. Allow minor omissions listed under allowedOmissions. Weight coverage of items according to domain weights supplied by the user (higher-weight domains should be present and precise).
